Note that the discrepancy between 2025 Revenue, Total Expenses and Operating Profit is due to Cost of Revenue. This figure is not reported in the table, as management did not provide any guidance on it at IPO. Cost of Revenue was $32.4 Mln in 2025, resulting in a gross profit of ~$6.2 Mln.

In my view, 2025 was a transitional year for Blaize. What matters in the context of these earnings is that the company has proven it can scale its products, and meet its own guidance. 2026 guidance also confirms a figure close to the upper end of IPO guidance, which is reassuring. In that sense, a significant risk to the company (that their product could not find a market fit) is now gone.

Looking ahead: profitability is now the key question mark

The company, in line with its IPO guidance, does not plan to achieve profitability in 2026. I am not particularly worried about this, because BZAI is focused on scaling their business. The company reports the three below executional priorities for 2026:

  1. Launching the AI Services platform in the second quarter to enable API-based AI capabilities and recurring revenue models.

  2. Expanding AI services and application delivery to support production-oriented, revenue generating deployments.

  3. Converting pipeline and partner-driven engagements into revenue across infrastructure and application use cases.

I see these priorities as logical and strategic, as Blaize needs to achieve scale before it can think about margins. The first priority in this regard is particularly important, as it will switch their business model from selling Edge AI chips to a recurring revenue model via their AI Services platform.

Yet, I have no doubt that the market will soon shift its focus from assessing the validity of Blaize’s solutions to whether the business model is sustainable and profitable, and to what extent. The company has a long way to go to prove it can reach first break-even, and then levels of marginalities that are typical in AI hardware companies. For reference, Nvidia Corporation (NVDA)’s gross margins are in the ~75% region.

At these prices, Blaize is an asymmetric bet on Edge AI

Blaize currently trades at a market capitalization of ~$170 Million. That’s a small premium relative to 2026 revenue guidance of ~$130 Million. Clearly, there are still significant risks involved in a trade with Blaize (which I will cover shortly), mostly involving the long term marginality of its business.

However, I think my original price target of ~12$ per share remains roughly valid. Given the company is not profitable yet, my valuation model is based purely on a P/S ratio.

Assuming a P/S ratio of 10 and 2026 guidance revenue, the company could be worth ~$1.3 billion ($130 Mln * 10). That would be equivalent to $12.62 per share, without accounting for any share dilution. Blaize plans to go from 103M to 150 Mln shares in 2026 (equivalent to issuing ~46% more shares). Accounting for that dilution, the price target would be ~$9 per share.

Ultimately, I think that if Blaize proves the sustainability of its business model, a $12 price target is still achievable in the mid-term, once the need for diluting shareholders diminishes.

A note on Blaize’s positioning within the Edge AI industry

In my very first article about BZAI, I outlined in detail what Edge AI is and what role Blaize could play in it. In a nutshell, Blaize's proprietary GSP architecture is specifically strong in graph-based AI workloads (like object detection across video streams), delivering real-time processing across multiple camera feeds at very low power. The company’s full-stack approach combines hardware and software, and it mirrors the Nvidia playbook of creating ecosystem lock-in.

Whether or not Blaize’s ecosystem can deliver a highly profitable, highly scalable business model remains to be seen. While we are just at the beginnings of scaling up, I am reassured by the quantity of deals Blaize announced since the end of 2025:

  • A strategic collaboration with Nokia Oyj (NOK) on a joint AI inference platform development.

  • The planned establishment of an AI innovation hub and R&D center in India, supporting applied AI pilots and implementations initiatives across mining safety, smart cities, and agriculture, with real-time monitoring of operations.

  • A partnership in South Korea with GSIL, with the objective of co-developing Physical AI solutions for industrial safety, enabling real-time risk detection, with pilots in Korea’s manufacturing environments.

Blaize also recently appointed ex-Cisco Stephen Patak as Chief Revenue Officer. The company mentions he will “scale AI deployments across public and private sector markets.” This is yet another development that I find particularly interesting as it gives credibility to the company’s go-to-market strategy.

Risks: profitability, dilution, competition and risk management

Despite its promising 2025 figures, Blaize still has a long road ahead. The company needs to keep scaling their business, while simultaneously proving their model is profitable to an extent comparable to that of far more established AI hardware players. Personally, I think that only once Blaize proves traction on both top and bottom line, the market will start assigning a premium valuation to this company.

In this sense, I think it’s likely shareholders could be stuck with a company trading at depressed valuation for the entirety of 2026, as the company doesn’t anticipate reaching profitability just yet.

This is especially likely considering the downward pressure on the stock coming from shares dilution that management forecasts for 2026. Blaize mentions $34.4 Mln in stock-based compensation, and issuance of 46% more shares for a total of 150 Mln shares by end of year.

Competition is another key factor of risk. Blaize effectively competes both with other niche players as well as established AI microchip giants like Nvidia, which are developing their own solutions targeting Edge AI applications.

More direct competitors include Hailo, an Israeli microchip manufacturer and Mobileye Global Inc (MBLY), a listed company that has been discussed for its role in self-driving mobility. The table below, taken from a study on microprocessors, outlines the key differences between selected microchips from these players.

A final element of risk concerns risk management within one’s portfolio. Blaize is a high-stake bet, and as such I do not recommend anyone to open a sizable position, even if they are bullish on the stock. The risk-reward profile for a micro cap like BZAI simply does not, in my view, warrant building a significant position.

Conclusion: why I see BZAI as a Strong Buy

Once again I find myself reiterating a Strong Buy for BZAI, as I think this company represents an asymmetric bet on a new niche AI player.

My bullish thesis is fairly simple at its core, and I would summarize it as follows:

  • After its IPO, the company guided $19M - $50M in revenue for 2025 and $105Mln - $140Mln for 2026.

  • Blaize managed to beat the guidance’s midpoint for 2025, and for 2026 they are guiding at the upper end of their original IPO estimates.

  • This, in my view, means that Blaize’s products are indeed marketable, while the stock does not reflect this reality and the company remains priced in as if it were to go bankrupt.

After 2025, the market may shift from focusing on revenue to assessing the long term sustainability of Blaize’s business model when it comes to profitability. The company is pursuing an “ecosystem” strategy similar to Nvidia’s that has the potential to deliver. However, competition abounds and there are further risks related to shareholders dilution.

Ultimately, this is a mid-term high-stake bet. I see a ~10X upside from current price levels if Blaize manages to prove their business model in the upcoming quarters.

data.title: Interparfums: Planting Seeds In 2026 To Reap Rewards In 2027
data.description:
The current year may be quiet or constructive for Interparfums, Inc. (IPAR) due to the impact of tariffs and marketing spending on newly licensed brands. However, IPAR's strategy to expand its owned brand, Solferino, and acquire new licenses makes 2026 a year for accumulation and monitoring the stock's performance before bearing fruit next year.

IPAR operates through obtaining the licenses and rights to use popular brand names for producing perfumes such as Guess and Montblanc, as the company designs the perfume formula and bottle shape and manufactures it using third-party factories. In return, they pay a sales percentage to the original brand owner.

The Financial Picture

The company closed the year with its best fourth quarter, as it achieved sales of $386 million, which is an excellent number given the sector's slowdown during this period. The full-year sales were $1.49 billion, with a 2% increase YoY, which exceeds the previous guidance of $1.47 billion and confirms the resilience of demand for its products.

The operating margin also decreased slightly by about 80 basis points to settle at 18.2%, driven by higher SG&A expenses. However, I believe this is a short-term compression as a result of marketing costs of upcoming brand licenses such as Off-White and Longchamp, alongside the expansion of its owned brand, Solferino.

Importantly, the performance of individual brands was impressive and proves the company's distribution strategy is successful, as Lacoste's sales in 2025 alone reached $108 million with growth of 28%, and Roberto Cavalli's sales increased by 33%, meaning that the company has the secret sauce and the ability to redesign the fragrance identity that suits each brand, which raises its sales exceptionally in record time.

The management expects sales of around $1.48 billion during 2026, which is a slight decline compared to 2025. EPS is also expected to decrease by about 5%, prompting some institutions to reassess the stock in the short term. However, there are some reasons that explain this decline, including the company's substantial investments in advertising and designing new perfumes mentioned earlier, plus the additional costs of $12.8 million resulting from tariffs; however, the management may try to offset these costs through gradual price increases, although this process may take some time.

The upcoming launches - particularly the Off-White brand, which has a high popularity among millennials and Gen Z - can open a completely new segment of consumers and achieve exceptional profits. The company indicated that it is going to launch it along with Longchamp in 2027.

In addition, IPAR aims to diversify income sources through expanding the number of outlets selling its luxury brand, Solferino. It began selling it through Bloomingdale's stores but plans to aggressively increase its doors from 40 to 90 doors in the first half of 2026 and aspires to reach 500 doors by 2030. This is a milestone step; its profit margins would be much higher than the other brands it does not own, as it will retain all of the profits.

The Competition & Valuation

IPAR owns around 5% of the global perfume market, which has fierce competition from major players such as L'Oréal, Estee Lauder, and Coty. However, IPAR concentrates its focus on just perfumes and managing licenses efficiently, avoiding the problems of direct manufacturing, plus it enjoys a positive operating margin and cash of $295.2 million, unlike Estee Lauder, which suffers a significant decline in the Asian market, especially China, or Coty, which is burdened by high debt obligations, straining its balance sheet.

IPAR's TTM EBITDA is $295.6 million and trades at an EV/EBITDA multiple of around 10x, while L'Oréal has a multiple of 16x due to its well-established position, unlike Coty, which trades at a low multiple of 6x due to its high debts. If IPAR succeeds in doubling Solferino customers and attracting new segments after increasing points of sale from 40 to 90, this could push the market to revalue the stock at higher multiples. I assume a conservative multiple of 12x, as the aggressive rollout of Solferino will shift the company's revenues toward higher-margin and fully owned sales, giving a premium over the current valuation. Therefore, its target EV could reach $3.5 billion. By adding cash reserves of $295 million, the market cap would come to $3.8 billion, representing potential upside of 35%.

Risks to Consider

The company submitted its 10-K annual report a few days ago, which revealed a material weakness in the risk assessment process, as the company didn't have a robust system to anticipate new risks that could impact its financial figures. The management confirmed that the current financial statements accurately reflect the company's financial position. Although this seems to be an administrative and compliance hurdle rather than an accounting issue, investors should monitor the company's progress in closing this control gap.

Also, about half of the company's revenues come in euros. Consequently, it is exposed to FX fluctuations, as a large portion of the company's costs are paid in euros. As a result, a stronger euro may pressure margins, even if reported revenues benefit from favorable USD-EUR translation, while the company can offset the difference by passing it to the customer and raising prices, but this might decrease its sales.

Final Thoughts

It appears that the current year will be more investment-focused for the company, with the benefits expected to materialize starting in 2027, which is expected to be a year for commercial distributions of new brands and also shows the impact of price increases that aim to restore margins that have been pressured due to tariffs. During the upcoming quarters, investors should monitor Solferino's progress, as this is the catalyst that could change the company's valuation from being a licensing company to a fashion and luxury house. However, the stock may experience a short-term decline and represent a better entry point as a result of the current market uncertainty, driven by geopolitical tensions and high oil prices, which could lead to inflationary effects or changes in exchange rates.

data.title: Oracle: Multicloud A Recurring Revenue Engine
data.description:
Introduction

Oracle Corporation (ORCL) is attempting to build a structurally different cloud business model that will be successful regardless of which hyperscaler (AWS, Azure, or Google Cloud) their customers use. To achieve this, Oracle has taken its Exadata hardware and is now physically deploying it into the data centers of all three hyperscalers. With physical deployments in place, Oracle has successfully transitioned what was once an "installed base" for defense into a "growth engine" for offense -- with multicloud database usage growing at 531% YoY. Also, Oracle reported an 84% increase in cloud infrastructure revenue and a $553 billion contracted backlog. Further, the company is 41% owned by its founders, which aligns with my investment view. I, therefore, assign a Buy rating on ORCL with a fair value of $244.20 based on discounted cash flow analysis. The estimated fair value represents a potential upside of 59.7% above the stock price of $152.90 (as of writing).

Company Overview

Oracle Corporation is the world’s largest enterprise database vendor and a rapidly expanding cloud infrastructure provider. Approximately 430,000 clients in 175+ countries are served by the company through 3 segments: OCI (cloud infrastructure); Fusion ERP, NetSuite, and Industry SaaS (Cloud Applications); and On-Premise Licensing & Support. The company generated $57.4 billion in revenue in FY2025, with management guiding to approximately $67 billion for FY2026. Following Q3 2026 results, the cloud now accounts for over 50% of total revenue. Larry Ellison is Executive Chairman and CTO and owns 41% of the company. This makes Oracle one of the few large-cap technology companies that is so "founder aligned."

Q3 2026 Results

Q3 was a pivotal quarter for Oracle. It was the first time since 2007 that the company had achieved 20%+ organic revenue and non-GAAP EPS growth for a single quarter. The company’s revenue increased to $17.2 billion (+22% YoY) and its cloud revenue to $8.9 billion (+44%), which is now about 52% of total revenue. Growth was strongest in AI Infrastructure (up 243%) and Multicloud Database Consumption (up 531%). Nevertheless, the quality of the growth needs to be evaluated. For example, while software revenue rose by only 3% in USD, it fell by 1% in constant currency. In other words, there was a decline of 1% in constant currency. Thus, the decline in on-premises revenues continues. Over the last twelve months, Oracle has reported $13.18 billion in negative free cash flow.

Q4 2026 Outlook

Management expects Q4 FY2026 to deliver an increase of 18-20% for total revenue on a constant currency basis (19-21% in U.S. dollars), which represents around $18.5-19 billion in Q4 revenue. This will be consistent with management's prior guidance of $67 billion for FY2026. Management also expects a non-GAAP earnings per share (EPS) of $1.99 in Q4, which represents continued margin expansion. For FY2027, management has raised its revenue guidance to $90 billion, which represents a dramatic acceleration to $23 billion in quarterly revenue run rate by mid-calendar year 2027. The Q4 fiscal year 2026 calendar has several catalysts. Oracle has agreed to acquire a 15% equity interest in the newly independent TikTok U.S.

Multicloud Adoption: A Recurring Growth Engine

In the fourth quarter of 2023, Oracle deployed its Exadata Database Service—its enterprise-grade managed database running on Oracle Cloud Infrastructure—natively within Azure data centers. It now offers it in 33 regions. It also offers the database on Google Cloud, and this was made available in seven regions at launch. At least ten additional regions are planned. The AWS version reached General Availability (GA) in mid-July 2025 and plans to be in twenty-two regions by the end of the current quarter. This isn't just about licensing agreements; instead, Oracle will physically deploy its own Exadata hardware—and run its own cloud software stack—into the data centers of AWS, Azure, and Google. That means customers can purchase the product directly from the hyperscalers' marketplaces, and it counts toward their existing cloud commitments. The friction to adopt the solution has been virtually eliminated due to the fact that over 430,000 companies are currently using an Oracle database.

Why would the hyperscalers provide a competitor with space to deploy their infrastructure? The reason is that the Oracle Database is used in many of the enterprise applications that make up the core of most business operations. For example, ERP, financials, and transactional workloads. Therefore, the hyperscalers were losing all the revenue associated with migrating those entire clouds to their platform. However, they still capture the overall migration revenue. Oracle captures the database layer. However, there is an asymmetry. Once the database runs on Oracle hardware inside a hyperscaler, it creates a gravitational force that causes the hyperscaler to expand consumption of Oracle products, not reduce them. Data gravity compounds.

The major factor that has resulted in the market significantly undervaluing the potential opportunity represented by Oracle's multicloud partnerships. The reason is due to the fact that until recently, the company had an extremely small revenue base from multicloud databases - with the first quarter (Q1) of fiscal year 2026 seeing an increase of 1,529% in multicloud database usage based on what can be considered a "rounding error" previously. Although there was an additional 531% increase in usage during the third quarter (Q3), the total estimate of this amount would represent approximately $150-$450 million per quarter. This is an extremely low percentage of Oracle's overall cloud revenue of $8.9 billion.

The Migration Funnel: Sizing the Opportunity

Installed Base Segmentation

The 430,000 number is an overall number of customers using any product (database, middleware, application, hardware, NetSuite, or Java) from Oracle, not just database. Third-party information suggests that approximately 63% of Oracle's customers are small businesses, 23% are mid-sized businesses, and 14% are large enterprises, based on their annual revenues.

License support revenue has remained relatively flat—$19.4 billion in FY2023, $19.6 billion in FY2024, and $19.5 billion in FY2025—representing the long-term annuity stream from the on-premise installed base. This represents the long-term annuity stream from the on-premise installed base paying 22% of net license fees every year in required support. The most recent quarterly figures show cloud infrastructure (IaaS) generated $4.9 billion in Q3 FY2026, or +84% vs. the same period last year; cloud applications (SaaS) generated $4.0 billion in Q3 FY2026, or +13%; while the total cloud revenue generated $8.9 billion in Q3 FY2026, or +44%.

Oracle does not disclose multicloud database revenue as a standalone figure. However, the 10-Q filed March 11, 2026, provides a disaggregation of "cloud services and license support" into two ecosystems. For Q3 FY2026, Infrastructure cloud services and license support totaled $6,196 million, while applications cloud services and license support totaled $4,811 million—combining to $11,007 million in total cloud services and license support.

Within the infrastructure ecosystem, two sub-streams exist: cloud infrastructure (IaaS) and infrastructure license support. The earnings release separately reported IaaS revenue at $4,900 million (+84% YoY) and cloud applications (SaaS) at $4,000 million (+13%), totaling $8,900 million in cloud revenue. Subtracting these from the $11,007 million total cloud services and license support implies approximately $2,107 million in combined license support (applications + infrastructure) for Q3.

Oracle's earnings call disclosed that cloud database revenue (a subset within IaaS) grew 35% in Q3. If we assume cloud database represents roughly 20-30% of the $4,900 million (IaaS) line—consistent with database historically being Oracle's core offering but now sharing the segment with GPU/AI compute—that yields approximately $980 million–$1,470 million in quarterly cloud database revenue. Multicloud database (the portion running inside AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud data centers) is a further subset. Management reported 531% YoY growth for this line. I assume multicloud revenues to be in an approximate range of $150 million to $450 million. The wide range reflects a sort of opacity around this metric—a common strategy when the absolute base remains small relative to headline growth rates.

The Non-Consensus Claim: What the Market Is Missing

On Wall Street, they model the cloud growth of Oracle under a single blended trajectory -- IaaS + SaaS + Database. There is no public analyst that models multicloud database revenue as its own separate line item. Instead, analysts embed the contribution within their overall IaaS estimates. That is important because multicloud databases have fundamentally different economics than renting GPUs.

Here are my thoughts on why the consensus has it wrong. AI Infrastructure rental (GPUs) carries about a 32% gross margin. The initial capital expenditures are huge. The number of large-scale customers (OpenAI, xAI, Meta) that use this service is very small. As the supply of GPUs normalizes, the service will face commodity price pressure. In contrast, multicloud database services carry gross margins ranging from 60% to 80%. The initial capital expenditures for the hyperscaler to host the database service are virtually nothing. The hyperscalers monetize a base of over 430,000 customers who currently have near-zero acquisition cost. Additionally, there are significant switching costs that have compounded for decades.

The consensus views Oracle's growth through megadeals and successful capex execution. Yet the stock trades at a forward GAAP PEG ratio of 0.98 as of writing—below Microsoft (MSFT) at 1.74, SAP at 2.05, and Amazon (AMZN) at 1.45—suggesting the market is pricing Oracle's growth potential at a discount to peers. The multicloud database strategy explains why that discount is unwarranted: it monetizes an installed base of customers that already exists, through distribution channels that have already been built, with switching costs that have compounded for decades. There is no need for Oracle to acquire new customers; it only needs to migrate workloads to the cloud.

Valuation

The model uses a 10-year DCF with a growth exit methodology. Revenue growth assumptions are front-loaded to reflect Oracle's near-term contract visibility: 17% in FY2026 (consistent with the $67 billion guidance), 34% in FY2027 (per management's raised $90 billion target), and 18% in FY2028 as the $553 billion (RPO) backlog starts converting to recognized revenue. Growth then decelerates to 12% by FY2029, 8% by FY2030, and tapers to 5% by FY2033-2035 as the AI infrastructure buildout matures and Oracle transitions toward steady-state cloud operations. By FY2035, the model projects revenue of approximately $169 billion and net profit of $48.9 billion, representing a 29% net margin—reflecting the structural margin expansion expected as lower-margin AI infrastructure capex normalizes and higher-margin cloud database and SaaS services scale.

The WACC of 7.80% is derived from a cost of equity range of 6.29%-10.95% and a cost of debt of 4.00%-4.94%. The model assumes a debt-to-equity ratio of 0.21. Total financial debt stands at approximately $134.6 billion against stockholders' equity of roughly $15.9 billion as of Q3 FY2026, yielding a ratio closer to 8.5x.

The terminal growth rate of 3.0% is consistent with long-term nominal GDP growth, reflecting the assumption that Oracle's revenue growth will converge with the broader economy beyond the explicit forecast period. Capex is assumed to normalize at 16.6% of revenue across the projection period, down from the current 37%, as Oracle completes its data center buildout phase.

The present value of projected free cash flows over the 10-year forecast period totals $258,137 million, representing 31.3% of enterprise value. The terminal value, calculated using the 3.0% perpetuity growth rate and 7.80% (WACC), is $566,501 million—representing 68.7% of enterprise value. Combined, these yield an enterprise value of $824,637 million. Subtracting the current net debt of $123,033 million (inclusive of operating and finance lease obligations per the Q3 FY2026 balance sheet) and dividing by 2,873 million diluted shares outstanding produces an equity value per share of $244.20, representing 59.7% upside from the current stock price of $152.90.

The FCF trajectory reflects Oracle's current investment phase: free cash flow begins at $4.1 billion in the stub FY2026 period, rising to $23.5 billion by FY2027 as revenue scales against a normalizing capex base. By FY2035, FCF is projected to reach $56.0 billion, driven by the margin differential between the high-growth infrastructure segment and the scaling of higher-margin cloud database services. This dramatic FCF expansion from $4.1 billion to $56.0 billion over the forecast period is primarily a function of capex declining from 37% of revenue to 16.6%, while operating margins remain stable in the 42-43% range (non-GAAP).

The sensitivity analysis demonstrates the range of outcomes under different assumptions. At the low end—using a 9.79% WACC and 2.0% terminal growth rate—the fair value falls to $141.29, representing 7.6% downside from the current price. At the high end—using a 5.82% WACC and 4.0% terminal growth rate—the fair value rises to $679.05, representing 344% upside. The base case of $244.20 sits in the lower third of this range, reflecting the conservative WACC and terminal growth assumptions.

Competitive Landscape

Oracle’s competitive position has been based on a shrinking but still very large moat. The database franchise—historically the "crown jewel" for Oracle—is actually being eroded: 55.6% of developers now use PostgreSQL, compared to 10.6% who use Oracle, and all three hyperscalers are creating their own enterprise-grade managed PostgreSQL services specifically as alternatives to using Oracle databases. Fortune 500 companies have begun to execute real migrations, not just evaluate them. However, due to both the complexity of PL/SQL codebases and the high level of dependency on middleware, displacing Oracle will take multiple years. Oracle’s multicloud database strategy (which has seen consumption grow by 531% YoY) allows users to run Oracle databases in AWS, Azure, and Google data centers. This eliminates the trigger that would have caused a customer to migrate.

Risks

Oracle is also exposed to customer concentration risks. A large portion (approximately 54%) of its $553 billion of remaining performance obligations (RPO) is due to one single contract for Stargate. OpenAI has yet to achieve profitability, as it is expected to lose approximately $9 billion. The company has reduced that exposure by using a multicloud database model. This allows it to disperse its revenue among a large number of established enterprises (currently over 430,000). These companies have virtually no cost associated with acquiring them, so the risk profile is very different from having all of your revenue concentrated into a few large AI deals.

There is also meaningful uncertainty about whether the full $553 billion in RPO will convert into recognized revenue and, ultimately, free cash flow. The backlog is heavily concentrated among a small cohort of AI infrastructure customers, several of whom are pre-profitability startups. OpenAI, which signed a roughly $300 billion cloud commitment through the Stargate partnership, does not expect sustained profitability until 2029. The ability to fund multi-year cloud commitments of this magnitude depends on continued external financing that is not yet fully secured. xAI, another significant OCI customer, faces similar funding dependencies. If even a fraction of these commitments are renegotiated, deferred, or cancelled, Oracle's revenue trajectory and free cash flow conversion could fall meaningfully below current projections. Investors should therefore discount the $553 billion RPO figure by the probability that not all contracted obligations will convert on schedule.

Conclusion

Although the forward P/E ratio of about 20x for Oracle is slightly higher than the median of ~18x for the systems software sector—and therefore is not what I would call cheap—the Quant System from Seeking Alpha shows a different picture: the A− Grade for EPS revisions reflects the opinion of 47 analysts who have raised their estimates after Q3. The growth numbers for Oracle are excellent—22% year-over-year growth in revenues, 84% year-over-year growth in cloud infrastructure, and $553 billion in RPO. In addition to its great growth numbers, Oracle's profitability is also excellent, with gross margins of 67% and operating margins of 32%. My Buy thesis is based on my specific belief that one aspect of Oracle's business is underestimated and that the multicloud database strategy will convert Oracle's highest-margin business line (60-80% gross margins) to a recurring cloud revenue stream with zero customer acquisition cost. The DCF-derived fair value for Oracle of $244.20 represents a 59.7% upside to the current share price (as of writing). It captures the embedded optionality of a $20 billion database franchise migrating to the cloud using the infrastructure provided by the hyperscalers.

data.title: Palantir: Defending Its High-Growth Premium
data.description:
Palantir Technologies Inc. (PLTR) is retaining its strong adoption momentum with the Maven Smart System (MSS), an AI-powered defense platform developed by Palantir that integrates data from diverse sources, such as satellites and drones, to enhance military situational awareness, target identification, and logistics. Further, it accelerates sensor-to-shooter loops for the U.S. military and NATO. Recently, Maven was designated as a Pentagon Program of Record, solidifying its position for long-term growth as it is more deeply utilized across military branches.

In addition to increased government utilization, Palantir has consistently grown its commercial customer base along with its average revenue per customer, nearly $900k per customer. Given the strong momentum and government & commercial tailwinds, I am upgrading PLTR shares to a Strong Buy rating with a price target of $255/share at 59.98x eFY27 price/sales.

You can review my previous coverage of Palantir here.

Optimism is driven by continued top-line acceleration and stronger-than-expected margins.

Palantir Technologies Operational Update

Palantir is currently facing a deep technical dilemma with the Trump administration “blacklisting” Anthropic (ANTHRO), suggesting that the AI development company is a supply chain risk and that defense tech companies should no longer use Claude. Setting the stage, I don’t believe Claude is in the wrong here; the firm stood its ground against its application to be used for fully autonomous weapons and for mass surveillance on American citizens. Nonetheless, President Trump provided agencies a 6-month phase-out period to decouple from Claude and begin using peer models like OpenAI (OPENAI).

The transition away from Claude may impact defense contractors like Palantir, which has established an embedded partnership over the last year and a half. The partnership was established to be layered into Palantir’s AI Platform [AIP] through Amazon Web Services (AMZN) as the facilitator. The partnership deepened with the establishment of FedStart, a program designed to accelerate the process to becoming compliant with FedRAMP, a requirement to contract or sell technology and other services to the Federal Government.

Despite the conflict between the Trump administration and Anthropic, Palantir has indicated that it will continue to partner with Anthropic’s AI models. While transitioning to peer AI models is perfectly feasible, this could potentially disrupt a large proportion of Palantir’s business over the coming quarters as applications will need to be rewritten to facilitate other models.

In March 2026, Deputy Secretary of Defense Steve Feinberg announced that Maven Smart System will become an official program of record, solidifying Palantir’s leadership in weapons-targeting technology for the U.S. military. As a result of the designation, Maven adoption will be streamlined across all branches of the military, providing Palantir with long-term funding. While the program is presently available across military branches, I believe the major takeaway is that it will be more deeply deployed and embedded across more military systems.

The challenge at hand is that Maven leverages Anthropic’s Claude, which puts the DoW and Palantir in a peculiar position of needing Maven to both operate as intended and phase out Claude with a replacement model to operate at the same quality of performance. Overall, I’m skeptical about whether Claude will be fully decoupled from Palantir’s platform given the brevity of reasoning behind the decision. I suspect that the “blacklisting” will be short-lived and will be reversed in the coming months. My argument is towards functionality; if an alternative model doesn’t perform at the same level as Claude, the DoW’s argument towards removing Claude may diminish.

Deepening its position in the DoW, Palantir recently announced a partnership with Anduril to develop the software for the Golden Dome program's antimissile shield initiative.

Further embedding itself in the defense industry, Palantir announced a multiyear partnership expansion with GE Aerospace (GE) following a 2024 pilot program to improve the U.S. Air Force’s aircraft part supply chain. The partnership has since expanded providing insights into full supply chain, maintenance, repair, and overhaul [MRO] orchestration, and new engine production.

In Q4 ’25, Palantir completed a live-fire exercise with Maven through an unmanned aerial vehicle [UAV] using Maven Edge. I have reason to believe that the exercise may have leveraged Red Cat Holdings (RCAT) UAVs given the two firms’ historical partnership to equip Black Widow with autonomous Visual Navigation [VNav] software and Warp Speed for GPS-compromised environments. Red Cat announced back in October 2025 that Black Widow had completed flight testing using Palantir’s VNav, while not providing solidified proof of use in the live fire exercise; this may heavily suggest that Red Cat’s drones were utilized.

An appealing factor is that Red Cat is developing a shipyard to produce unmanned surface vehicles [USVs] as oceanic drones, a potential growth market given the current events in the Middle East, particularly in the Strait of Hormuz. These USVs can be outfitted with short-range and long-range defense systems, which could potentially be orchestrated by Palantir Maven.

While the USV program is in early development, production is expected to amount to 100+ by the end of 2026 before ramping up to thousands annually. As for Black Widow, production is expected to ramp up to 1,000 units per month in e1h26, up 6.6x from current production.

Palantir Technologies Financial Position & Forecast

Palantir closed FY25 with exceptionally strong growth, delivering another quarter of accelerated growth. Without diluting margins, Palantir continued to expand the top-line while bolstering the adjusted operating margin to deliver a “Rule Of” 127% in q4’25, up from 114% in the previous quarter. Looking ahead, I’m forecasting the trend to continue through eFY26 before tapering off in eFY27 as growth becomes more saturated and normalized.

For Q1 ’26, I’m forecasting Palantir to generate $1.54b in net revenue with an adjusted EPS of $0.35/share. Given the post-Q4 ’25 momentum with its recently announced program of record for Maven and its strategic partnership with GE Aerospace, I believe growth may be above the curve for eFY26, particularly driven by government-related contracts. With the Iranian war seeing deeper mobilization, I believe Palantir’s role may become substantially more critical as part of the modern battlefield, particularly as drones and missiles are used target critical energy infrastructure, nuclear research centers, and radar detection systems in the Middle East.

Risks Related to Palantir Technologies

Bull Case

Palantir is playing a critical role in the Department of War that will deepen with Maven becoming a program of record, establishing a long-term stream of revenue. Commercial adoption continues to grow at a heightened rate while revenue per customer grows.

The potential for increased deployment of drone technology across the military branches may drive growth for Palantir’s orchestration tools.

Bear Case

While top-line growth is expected to continue at an accelerated rate for eFY26, I believe a more normalized rate could influence the market’s perception of Palantir’s growth trajectory.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

PLTR shares are currently trading at a rich premium of 89x price/sales. While shares are priced for high growth, the premium has come off its recent high of closer to 125x price/sales last seen in November 2025.

While shares remain expensive, forward growth estimates may support this elevated price point and deliver continued share price expansion over the coming years. Overall, I believe operations will catch up to the market capitalization over time, particularly when considering its deepening ties to the DoW, increased adoption by commercial customers, and increased average revenue per customer. Given these factors, I am upgrading PLTR shares to a Strong Buy rating with a price target of $255/share at 59.98x eFY27 price/sales.

Using the model: the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm’s “financial position” section and ties it to the stock’s historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle with the blue-sky scenario being the stock’s peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company’s stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative market capitalization and relative multiple.

data.title: TOPW: Weekly Dividends From A Portfolio Of Large Cap Equities
data.description:
Introduction to the Roundhill Top WeeklyPay ETF

It used to be that investors had to purchase baskets of equities and when cash was needed they would just sell some of the holdings to harvest capital gains. It is no longer the case in today's environment. We have seen the massive rise of options ETFs which serve the purpose of harvesting gains from individual stocks and baskets of equities. Roundhill Investments has been at the forefront of this revolution, with a plethora of offerings in the space.

In today's article we are going to have a close look at the Roundhill Top WeeklyPay ETF (TOPW), its composition and structuring, as well as the best utilization for this complex instrument.

Russian doll composition

Let us first start with the objective for TOPW:

The Roundhill Top WeeklyPay™ ETF seeks to track the performance of the Solactive Roundhill WeeklyPay™ Universe Index. TOPW is designed to invest in WeeklyPay™ ETFs tied to the 25 largest U.S. companies by market capitalization.

Just like the image above, TOPW acts like a Russian doll - for those unfamiliar with the concept here is a brief explanation:

Russian dolls, known as Matryoshka dolls, are traditional Russian wooden nesting dolls created around 1890, representing iconic folk art and culture. They consist of a set of hand-painted, hollow, cylindrical wooden dolls of decreasing size placed one inside another.

Similar to Matryoshka dolls, TOPW contains a host of other Roundhill ETFs, namely single name option ETFs:

We can see that the holdings are single stock Roundhill weekly pay ETFs, built in a market-weighted fashion. The top-10 names account for roughly 90% of the fund, thus representing the main risk factors. But what exactly are those single name ETFs in there and what do they do?

Single name ETF concept

Retail investors love yield and the financial markets have obliged by creating the single stock ETF. A single stock ETF like the Roundhill NVDA WeeklyPay ETF (NVDW) for example, simply aims to extract stable dividends from the performance of the underlying stock, with a leverage factor on top:

The Roundhill NVDA WeeklyPay™ ETF (“NVDW”) is designed for investors seeking a combination of income and growth potential. NVDW aims to provide weekly distributions and calendar week returns, before fees and expenses, equal to 1.2 times (120%) the calendar week total return of Nvidia common shares (Nasdaq: NVDA). NVDW is an actively-managed ETF. There is no guarantee that the Fund will successfully provide returns that correspond to approximately 1.2 times (120%) the calendar week total return of common shares of NVDA. An investment in the Fund is not an investment in the underlying stock.

That leverage factor is 1.2x in the case of NVDW, and works well in up-trending markets:

The common equity from NVIDIA (NVDA) has a high correlation to NVDW, and please remember that the 1.2x factor is on a weekly basis, not longer time frames. We can see that on a 1-year time-frame the two securities have very similar returns (albeit one would expect a larger positive total return for NVDW).

NVDW achieves its goals via equity swaps:

The main holding in the underlying ETF is a weekly pay swap it has on the books. We like this structure because the funds that employ complex options strategies usually fail to deliver similar returns to the underlying stocks. An equity swap basically puts the structuring pressure on the counterparty (usually an investment bank) which manages that risk.

The leverage works against the ETF in down-trending markets:

The above chart plots the total return for Microsoft Corp (MSFT) and the Roundhill MSFT WeeklyPay ETF (MSFW). We can see that in a down market the Roundhill ETF posts a larger total negative return. Leverage works both ways.

All individual weekly pay ETFs have very high dividend rates, but unless there are substantial capital gains they tend to see NAV erosion:

On a one-year lookback NVDW has now seen a -13% decrease in NAV, although its total return is still positive. Only expect a stable or positive NAV in highly up-trending markets.

The purpose of the single name ETF is to extract dividends for holders automatically. By virtue of the weekly distributions, holders get large streams of income which represent capital gains in up-markets. Via this structure an investor does not have to sell underlying holdings, and basically does not need to worry about timing the market: the structure constantly harvests gains for them (weekly to be precise). Let us go through an example:

  • As an investor you put in $100 in NVDW
  • In an up-trending market you will get high weekly dividends
  • At the end of the year you will still have $100 in NVDW (no capital appreciation) while also sitting on $20 in dividends (in cash)
  • The structure has thus harvested for you capital gains

This type of ETF achieves two main goals:

  • Creating income from capital gains
  • Defeasance of the structure - if NVDA goes to zero tomorrow, the NVDW is only set to lose $80 ($100 minus the cash dividends) while NVDA holders lose the entire amount

Taking the single name ETF concept and creating a basket

TOPW is a basket of such single name ETFs, and represents a structured way of achieving the same goals as the single name wrapper, but for a basket of securities. We already saw from the composition that the ETF is market weighted, and its performance since inception is similar to other names:

We are comparing TOPW to the Invesco S&P 500 Top 50 ETF (XLG) and to the MicroSectors FANG+™ ETN (FNGS). Both funds have their top ten names represent a high percentage of the overall holdings, and their concentrations in individual stocks are similar to TOPW.

We can see a very close correlation in price performance, with TOPW and FNGS very highly correlated. XLG and FNGS however just hold outright equities, with no dividend component. For the above graph we are using the total return feature in order to include dividends, thus comparing overall performance.

The close correlation and total returns show in fact that TOPW just represents a structured way to extract dividends from a basket of equities, with the addition on the 1.2x gearing factor.

Some analytical details for the ETF are as follows:

  • AUM: $171 M
  • Expense ratio: 0.99%
  • Inception date: September 2025
  • SEC Yield: 56%
  • Payment period: weekly
  • ETF Type: passive
  • Reference index: Solactive Roundhill WeeklyPay Universe Index

The ETF is new, having come to market late in 2025.

Weekly pay ETFs work best in up-trending markets

As we have seen from the above, individual weekly pay ETFs and the aggregator TOPW, simply transform capital gains into dividends, albeit with a leverage factor. What happens though when there are no capital gains? If a stock moves lower (as we saw for Microsoft) then there are no capital gains to be had. But the single name ETF still disburses dividends. From where? From net asset value (NAV). This simply means you are getting your cash back, and when there is a recovery in the stock price you will have less of an upside than before because your notional amount has decreased substantially. Let us revisit the Microsoft example on a 1-year time-frame:

While the ETF total return is -30%, the price change is -44.9%, meaning an investor is close to only having half the exposure as a year ago. This translates into a much lower upside if a market recovery does happen. The longer the drawdown in MSFT and MSFW, the worse the NAV destruction for the holders. It all boils down to disbursing capital gains which do not exist anymore. This is a major weak point for this type of structure.

Investors should discount the dividend level completely

By now an informed investor already understands that the yield for this product is meaningless, since it represents a mere extraction of capital gains (or lack thereof) via the created structure. If we look at the latest composition of the weekly distribution for TOPW we get the following:

Roughly 44% of the distribution is composed of return of capital ('ROC') which in essence is a return of your own money. ROC accounts for NAV levels moving lower in a fund like TOPW. An investor can find the full list of Section 19a notices here.

We made a bold statement here saying that investors should discount the yield level completely for a product like TOPW because yields will fluctuate significantly, and as we can see from the table above they can be widely unsupported. Investors should look at total returns instead for aggregators like TOPW, and compare those total returns with simple baskets of the underlying equities.

Risks for TOPW

The main risk factor here is a prolonged recession. We have already established that TOPW experiences significant NAV erosion in a down-market. A prolonged recession would see a move lower in the prices of the underlying equities, as well as a large NAV destruction for the fund. Just look at the current factual data - the ETF is already down -33% on a price basis since inception, and we have not hit a recession yet. Do not underestimate the types of gaps lower in price one can see here during a recession.

The second largest risk factor is the embedded 1.2x leverage. While in bull markets the embedded leverage works for TOPW in generating gains, during recessionary periods it acts as another down lever. Leverage works both ways, and it can really bite during recessionary times.

A third risk factor which should be clear by now for investors is the yield/distribution rate. Never, ever make an investing decision based on yield only. TOPW only disburses capital gains from the underlying basket of equities, and via its weekly pay structuring creates large artificial annualized dividend yields. Investors should only look at total returns, and understand they have exposure to an underlying basket of individual equities, not a cash based fixed income product.

Key Advantages and Disadvantages

Advantages:

  • Structured way to extract weekly dividends from equities that might not have a yield
  • A 1.2x leverage factor which provides for upside in risk-on markets
  • A basket of large cap equities with inherent diversification
  • Attractive way to harvest gains and create an income stream during bull markets
  • The structure keeps de-risking and taking profits during long bull markets

Disadvantages:

  • Leverage can work against the fund in risk-off markets
  • The structure disburses 'capital gains' even when there are none to be had
  • Significant NAV erosion in down markets
  • Large portions of the distribution can be return of capital
  • The structure is designed to offer the 1.2x return for the underlying equities only on a weekly basis (longer time-frames will offer significantly different return profiles)

Conclusion

TOPW is an innovative ETF from Roundhill. The name aggregates individual equity ETFs that have weekly pay features and a 1.2x gearing factor. The top ten ETFs in the holdings account for over 90% of the fund, and are mainly large cap equities which come from the Magnificent 7 cohort and the top components of the S&P 500. TOPW ensures weekly distributions representing capital gains on the equities during bull markets. The ETF is a complex tool that solely transforms equity capital gains into dividends with a leverage factor, and can see very substantial NAV erosion during bear markets.

This article answers these three questions about TOPW:

  • Should investors focus on total return on yield when considering TOPW?
  • How should investors expect TOPW to perform in different market conditions?
  • What unique risks do TOPW investors face?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: W.P. Carey: I'm Buying Into The Turnaround
data.description:
Summary

After covering REITs like Realty Income (O) and VICI properties (VICI), I want to set my sights on W.P. Carey (WPC), which has potentially one of the most intriguing stories in the REIT sector, following its office portfolio divestiture and its dividend cut that has pushed the market to highly punish the stock. With that in mind, let’s dive into one of the best opportunities in the space.

Current Dynamics

I’ll first go through the latest earnings as to grasp the current financial story behind the REIT. WPC posted a double beat with revenue coming in at $444.55MM, a beat of $11.27MM and a 10.1% Y/Y increase. On the bottom line, Q4 FFO came out to $1.27, a beat of 3 cents. This was largely linked to the increase in investment volume through the year and the momentum is set to continue into 2026 as highlighted by the relatively strong guidance. For the full year, the REIT is expecting AFFO to come in between $5.13 & $5.23 with a total investment volume set to reach a maximum of $1.75B. This highlights that WPC is set to actually outpace peers like Realty Income.

On the broader side, WPC’s recent history was marked by the exit of its office assets which historically represented 15% of the company’s annualized base rent but increasingly acted as a valuation drag due to the work from home trend and the rising cost of office tenant improvements. Though it was relatively welcomed, the spin-off of these assets resulted in a 20% reduction in the dividend, a move to keep the payout ratio at a healthy level. Income investors drastically punished the stock, which has been on a recovering trend ever since. And in my opinion, this was overall a good move by the company based on the continued secular decline in the office space.

This dynamic has freed up capital that WPC is currently using toward industrial and warehouse properties, which now constitutes 68% of the overall volume, plus it allows WPC to surf on the onshoring manufacturing trends. This has increased the manufacturing share in new leasing to now 20% as firms are moving production of high-value tech inland to reduce geopolitical risks, and WPC is acquiring specialized manufacturing facilities under LT leases.

On another front, the e-commerce sector continues to act as a structural driver for logistics demand as operators continue to expand fulfillment capacity to meet the stringent standards of rapid delivery. The scarcity of top-tier industrial space supports WPC’s 98% occupancy rate and empowers the company to achieve high rent recapture rates when leases expire. I also like the focus on mission criticality, meaning that WOC is targeting properties that are key to the tenant’s operations and that would be massively disrupted to continue without it. Examples include facilities with heavy machinery, specialized power infrastructure and cold storage, which in a way trap tenants, as the costs of relocating these operations more than often outweigh the benefits of selling lower rent elsewhere.

WPC also has around 35% of its portfolio located in Europe, providing two key advantages: first, a bigger opportunity for sale-leaseback transactions, and second, a superior rent escalation structure, as in Europe these are generally uncapped, while in the US they are heavily linked to CPI. Furthermore, WPC’s structure allows for a relative hedge as management uses Eurobonds to match Euro rental income. If the Euro weakens against the USD, rental income will decrease, but the interest expense on the debt will also decrease, neutralizing the impact on AFFO.

Valuation

Though I am bullish on Realty Income and VICI, I believe that WPC can also be a strong contender to take a spot in your portfolio. It currently has a P/AFFO of 13.2, slightly lower than O but drastically lower than Agree Realty and Essential Properties Realty. This gap is notable considering the underlying AFFO growth for WPC that has been outpacing peers like NNN over the past year, and I believe that this is still linked to the conglomerate discount and the missing dividend track record. Talking about dividends, the yield is in line with other peers at a strong 5.37% that has grown by 3.72% over the past year.

I also did a scenario-based analysis that comprises a set of different cases. Though it only offers a slim upside to current levels, the dividend prices a relatively fair appreciation over the next year. The same could be said about a NAV-based valuation.

The core growth prospects are in mind, linked to the industrial and manufacturing pivot, which comprised 68% of the record $2.1B investment volume in 2025. There is also the formal launch of the Carey Tenant Solutions platform in early 2026 that represents a shift toward more active and relationship-driven growth. Instead of competing in high-priced public auctions, WPC will source off-market deals by funding expansions and redevelopments for its 371 existing tenants, and these will offer super economics.

Risks To Consider

First, the messy execution of getting out of the office space has damaged the credibility with income investors who relied on the 25-year streak of annual increases. Rebuilding trust takes years, which is why the company can stay discounted for a while, though I believe that this leaves opportunity for us as WPC now has to catch up to its peers that have a long track record.

Like most triple net REITs, WPC is highly rate-sensitive; a higher-for-longer interest rate environment will compress the spread between acquisition rates and the cost of capital. Moreover, the yield offered by the company is also compared to risk-free treasury yields, which might not entice investors if they are close to the same.

Conclusion

Through the pivot, I believe that W.P. Carey is now a better company without the troubled office segment that lets WPC focus on growth-oriented projects such as industrial and logistics opportunities. I also believe that the European exposure is a strength in this case, and thus I rate W.P. Carey as a Buy.

data.title: IWX: A Better Start To 2026 Amidst Market Turmoil
data.description:
2026 Outlook & Overview of IWX

The iShares Russell Top 200 Value ETF (IWX) is a passively managed exchange-traded fund (also known as an ETF) that invests in large-cap American-listed stocks. The point of this ETF is to measure the performance of large U.S. companies based on their fundamental size and weight, with the fund manager rebalancing and reviewing the fund quarterly or less for investment opportunities. In 2025, the fund returned around 16%, good in comparison to any other year, but below the S&P 500, other countries, and other value benchmarks. That said, amidst market turmoil, including the war in the Middle East and tariff rebuttals by the Supreme Court, IWX has held up pretty well in Q1 2026 and remains a decent investment vs. other passively managed peers. The fund has historically struggled against other large-cap funds that have some growth characteristics, along with also underperforming some of its value peers like iShares S&P 500 Value ETF (IVE), which doesn't screen out as many names as IWX does. Since my last article in January, the fund has done pretty well, and over the last 6 months, it has outperformed IVE and SPY.

Bank economists expected the U.S. market to do increasingly well in their end-of-year review, with all major bank analysts predicting another rise. I was a bit skeptical when all economists were lined up on one side of the trade, and Trump decided to shoot the market in the foot after a brief stint of recovery. From a fundamental point of view, Vanguard's 2026 outlook highlighted an imbalance, noting that U.S. growth stocks dominated by large-cap technology firms face higher expectations and competitive pressures that could hinder returns. In contrast, value stocks have demonstrated more consistent earnings growth but have been left for dead by investors over the past few years, which is why taking a deep dive into some options, like IWX, is important.

IWX's benchmark target is the Russell Top 200 Value Index. The index selects, ranks, and weights securities by fundamental measures like lower price-to-book ratios, lower sales-per-share historical growth, and lower forecasted growth relative to all securities in the Russell Top 200. This article will tell you whether it's worth investing with IWX or another asset manager/fund when thinking about investing in large-cap equities, or if you should just stick with SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY), which has a balance of growth and value, though it has been criticized recently for being too top-heavy. IWX is passively managed, which means that the fund managers don't make many trades and only rebalance every once in a while, versus active managers, who have the mandate to buy and sell stocks more often if they feel that there's a compelling investment opportunity.

Ultimately, if you simply want large-cap value exposure, IWD has been a better performer over one year, but over the last 6 months, IWX has looked solid. Given the recent value rebound, I think IWX is worth a cautious Buy as the market continues to see volatility.

Fund Breakdown

IWX was launched on September 22, 2009, by BlackRock after the financial crisis with a mandate to generally track large-cap U.S. stocks that exhibit value characteristics. In the aftermath of the financial crisis, fund managers were still reeling and investors were scarred, so these modified equity funds started to pop up. The index chooses and weights securities based on three fundamental metrics—lower price-to-book ratios, lower sales-per-share historical growth, and lower forecasted growth relative to all securities in the Russell Top 200. This approach looks to identify potential outperformers who are undervalued and are not too different from other large-cap value ETFs. The idea of the fund isn't bad, and I understand the perspective of having some value in the portfolio, but the fund has simply not performed well until recently.

The fund has been trading for years, with a NAV of ~$3.4 billion as of Mar. 24, 2026, and a daily volume of over 199K shares traded worth over $15 million. The portfolio turnover is around 18%, which tracks as the fund attempts to simply replicate an index based on a few value factors. And within the choppy market, the fund has remained resilient recently, beating IVE and SPY.

The fund employs a 0.2% yearly ETF expense ratio for fees to the asset manager — low and honestly reasonable given the differentiation and portfolio customization. Ultimately, the fund is decent, and I'm keeping my eye on it going forward.

Portfolio and Risk Profile

When comparing IWX's portfolio to peers, there are subtle differences in the portfolio breakdown, but not many. As of the recent fund facts on Mar. 23, 2026, the portfolio mostly resembles its index benchmark and overall captures the spirit of the large-cap value universe, with the main difference being a much smaller weighting in technology by ~15% and a higher weighting in financials by ~9%. Additionally, the equities in the fund are skewed in value, with a lower average P/B of 3x and a P/E of 21.4x. Historically, it's been kind of the worst of both worlds, as these ratios are only slightly lower than SPY and IVE, but the fund has underperformed quite a bit. But as value has become a safe haven, the fund has succeeded recently.

From a risk perspective, a current beta of 0.74 notes that the fund will have less volatility than the market—and that has borne out in past results and tracks for a value-based fund. When evaluating IWX's risk-adjusted returns, I analyzed the Sharpe ratio to see whether the fund has generated excess returns over the risk-free rate when adjusted for total volatility. Unfortunately, FNDA's Sharpe ratio for the fund is 1.04, above the score of 1+ to prove strong risk-adjusted returns - and in line with peers.

Bottom Line

The bottom line for this fund, in my opinion, is that it's a solid option that's held up amidst market madness. It's a pure play basket of a few large-cap value stocks and is very liquid. If investors want to have value in their portfolio, this ETF is fine. I will call it out that this has been rare, and value has been a laggard of SPY for decades now, but this fund meets the moment, and I think it is worth a small bet.
